s1_reasoning: The main update loop has low-to-moderate arithmetic intensity due to repeatedly reading A[j][k] with poor spatial locality (stride-N access in row-major layout) and repeatedly updating C[i][j] across k (read-modify-write). Without blocking, cache reuse is limited and performance is typically constrained by memory bandwidth and cache misses rather than peak FLOPs.

hotspots.reason: This is the O(N^2*M) rank-k update; it performs the vast majority of floating-point operations and repeatedly streams/updates the lower-triangular part of C while reading A. For typical PolyBench sizes, this dominates runtime.
O(N^2) pass over C; relatively small compared to the O(N^2*M) update but still touches a large memory region.

hotspots.location: print_array(): i-j printing loop (fprintf of C)
hotspots.estimated_time_percentage: 0-90% (depends on whether timing includes it / dump enabled)
hotspots.reason: If output is enabled and included in measured time, formatted I/O can dominate. In PolyBench, timing typically excludes this, but it can still dominate end-to-end wall time.

bottleneck_type.reasoning: The main update loop has low-to-moderate arithmetic intensity due to repeatedly reading A[j][k] with poor spatial locality (stride-N access in row-major layout) and repeatedly updating C[i][j] across k (read-modify-write). Without blocking, cache reuse is limited and performance is typically constrained by memory bandwidth and cache misses rather than peak FLOPs.

gpu_suitability.reasoning: SYRK is a dense linear algebra kernel with high parallelism and regular memory access when implemented with blocking; GPUs perform very well using optimized BLAS-3 routines. Naive porting of the given loops may be memory-inefficient, but mapping to cuBLAS/rocBLAS SYRK achieves high throughput.
gpu_suitability.recommended_approach: Use cuBLAS/rocBLAS syrk (or gemm) on device-resident A and C. Transfer A and C once, run syrk with uplo=lower, trans=no-trans, then (optionally) copy back only the needed triangle. For best performance, use column-major layouts expected by BLAS or adapt leading dimensions accordingly; rely on library tiling rather than custom kernels unless specialized fusion (beta scaling) is required.
